levels.description: Pod nginx-broken exits immediately after creation. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and check the STATUS and RESTARTS columns.
levels.hint1:
The pod is crashing — the container starts and exits right away. Read what it printed before dying:

  kubectl logs nginx-broken -n k8smissions --previous

The --previous flag reads from the last terminated container, not the current one (which may not have output yet).
levels.hint2:
The log says something like 'nginxzz: not found'. The container tried to run a command that does not exist inside the image.

Now look at the full pod spec to find where that command is configured:

  kubectl get pod nginx-broken -n k8smissions -o yaml

Look at spec.containers[0] carefully.
levels.hint3:
There is a 'command' field in spec.containers[0]. When 'command' is set, Kubernetes replaces the image's ENTRYPOINT with whatever you wrote there.

Open manifest.yaml and find this field.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The field `command: ["nginxzz"]` overrides the image's ENTRYPOINT. Instead of starting nginx, the container tries to run `nginxzz` — which does not exist anywhere in the image. It exits immediately with code 127 (command not found).

## Fix

Remove the `command` field from manifest.yaml:

```yaml
# delete this line:
command: ["nginxzz"]
```

Without `command`, the container uses the nginx image's default ENTRYPOINT (`nginx -g 'daemon off;'`).

## Why this matters

In a Dockerfile there are two fields: ENTRYPOINT (the executable) and CMD (default arguments). In Kubernetes, `command` maps to ENTRYPOINT and `args` maps to CMD. Setting `command` completely replaces the image's entrypoint — a common source of confusion when writing pod specs.

levels.description: Pod crashloop-pod is stuck in CrashLoopBackOff. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and watch the RESTARTS column climb.
levels.hint1:
CrashLoopBackOff means the container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes restarts it with an increasing delay (10s → 20s → 40s...). Read the output from the last crash:

  kubectl logs crashloop-pod -n k8smissions --previous

Without --previous you may see nothing — the new container just started.
levels.hint2:
The log shows something like '/scripts/start.sh: not found'. The container tried to execute a shell script that does not exist inside the busybox image.

Confirm what the container is actually trying to run:

  kubectl get pod crashloop-pod -n k8smissions -o yaml

Look at spec.containers[0].args
levels.hint3:
The `args` field contains `/scripts/start.sh`. This script does not exist in the busybox:1.36 image.

Fixing this is not just removing args — you also need to give the container a valid command to keep it running. Open manifest.yaml and look at what needs to change.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`args: [/scripts/start.sh]` tells the container to execute a script that does not exist inside the image. The container exits with code 127 (command not found). Kubernetes restarts it, the same thing happens, and the loop continues.

## Fix

Remove `args` and add a valid `command` that keeps the container alive:

```yaml
command:
- /bin/sh
- -c
- sleep 3600
```

## Why this matters

If you only remove `args` without adding `command`, busybox has no default process to run — it exits with code 0 immediately, which also causes restarts. A container must have a long-running foreground process to stay alive.

levels.description: Pod web-app is stuck in ImagePullBackOff and will never start. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions to confirm the status.
levels.hint1:
ImagePullBackOff means Kubernetes tried to pull the container image from the registry and failed. Get the exact error message:

  kubectl describe pod web-app -n k8smissions

Scroll to the Events section at the bottom of the output.
levels.hint2:
The Events say 'Failed to pull image'. Look at the image name being pulled. Does the tag after the colon actually exist on Docker Hub for nginx?

You can also check the image directly:

  kubectl get pod web-app -n k8smissions -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}'
levels.hint3: The image is `nginx:nonexistent-tag-xyz-123`. This tag does not exist on Docker Hub. The fix is in the `image` field inside spec.containers[0] in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`image: nginx:nonexistent-tag-xyz-123` — this tag does not exist. When Kubernetes tries to pull it, the registry returns a 404. After a few failed attempts it enters BackOff mode and stops retrying immediately.

## Fix

Change the image tag to one that exists:

```yaml
image: nginx:latest
```

## Why this matters

In production, using `latest` is discouraged — a new image version could be pulled unexpectedly and break your app. Prefer a specific version tag like `nginx:1.25.3` so the cluster always runs the exact version you tested with.

levels.description: Pod hungry-app was submitted but has been in Pending ever since — no node has been assigned to it. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Pending means the scheduler could not find a node that fits this pod. Read the scheduler's explanation:

  kubectl describe pod hungry-app -n k8smissions

Look at the Events section — why did the scheduler skip every node?
levels.hint2:
The Events say 'Insufficient memory' or 'Insufficient cpu'. The pod is requesting more resources than any node can provide.

See how much is actually available in the cluster:

  kubectl describe nodes | grep -A5 'Allocatable'
levels.hint3: Look at spec.containers[0].resources.requests in manifest.yaml. The values there are `memory: 999Gi` and `cpu: 999`. Compare these numbers to what you saw in node Allocatable.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`memory: 999Gi` and `cpu: 999` are larger than any node in the cluster can provide. The scheduler checks every node and finds none that can satisfy the request — so the pod stays Pending forever.

## Fix

Set realistic values in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    memory: "64Mi"
    cpu: "100m"
  limits:
    memory: "128Mi"
    cpu: "200m"
```

## Why this matters

`requests` is what the scheduler uses to decide where to place the pod — it guarantees that amount is available. `limits` is the ceiling the container cannot exceed. Both should reflect what the application actually needs.

levels.description: Pod env-pod starts but crashes almost immediately. Run kubectl logs env-pod -n k8smissions and read what the application says.
levels.hint1:
The pod is not failing to start — the container launches and then exits. That means the problem is inside the application, not Kubernetes config. Read the output:

  kubectl logs env-pod -n k8smissions

If the pod restarted and logs are gone:

  kubectl logs env-pod -n k8smissions --previous
levels.hint2:
The log says 'DATABASE_URL not set, exiting'. The application checks for an environment variable that is not there and exits with code 1.

See what environment variables are currently configured in the pod:

  kubectl get pod env-pod -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A10 'env'
levels.hint3: There is no `env` section under spec.containers[0]. The application requires `DATABASE_URL` to be injected as an environment variable. Find the right place to add it in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The container runs a shell script that checks `$DATABASE_URL`. If the variable is empty, it prints an error and calls `exit 1`. Since no `env` is defined in the pod spec, the variable is never set.

## Fix

Add the `env` section under the container in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
env:
- name: DATABASE_URL
  value: postgres://localhost/app
```

## Why this matters

In production, sensitive values like connection strings should never be hardcoded into a pod spec. They belong in a Secret, then referenced with `secretKeyRef`. For this lab, a plain value is fine.

levels.description: Resources were created but the validator cannot find them in the k8smissions namespace. Start with kubectl get pods -n k8smissions — is anything there?
levels.hint1:
If k8smissions is empty, the resources were probably created somewhere else. Check all namespaces at once:

  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
  kubectl get services --all-namespaces

Note which namespace client-app and backend-service actually ended up in.
levels.hint2:
The resources are in the `default` namespace. Now look at why:

  kubectl get pod client-app -n default -o yaml | grep 'namespace'

Then open manifest.yaml and find the same field.
levels.hint3:
Both resources in manifest.yaml have `namespace: default`. They need to be in `k8smissions`.

But editing manifest.yaml alone is not enough — the existing resources in `default` are still there. Think about what steps are needed.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

Both the Pod and the Service have `namespace: default` in their metadata. Kubernetes created them in `default`, not in `k8smissions` where the game expects them.

## Fix (two steps)

**Step 1** — Delete the misplaced resources:
```bash
kubectl delete pod client-app -n default
kubectl delete service backend-service -n default
```

**Step 2** — Fix the namespace in manifest.yaml for both resources:
```yaml
metadata:
  namespace: k8smissions
```
Then apply the file.

## Why this matters

Namespaces are isolation boundaries. A Service in namespace A cannot automatically reach Pods in namespace B (without a fully-qualified DNS name like `svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local`). Mismatched namespaces are a common mistake when moving resources between environments.

levels.description: Pod backend-app is Running and the Service exists — but traffic is not reaching the pod. Start with kubectl get endpoints backend-service -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
When a Service exists but traffic does not arrive, the first thing to check is whether the Service has any Endpoints:

  kubectl get endpoints backend-service -n k8smissions

If the Endpoints column shows `<none>`, the Service cannot find any matching pod. Services select pods using labels.
levels.hint2:
Check the labels on the pod:

  kubectl get pod backend-app -n k8smissions --show-labels

Now check the selector the Service uses to find pods:

  kubectl get service backend-service -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'selector'

Do these match?
levels.hint3:
The pod has label `app: backend`. The Service selector is looking for `app: frontend`. There is a mismatch — the Service cannot find the pod.

Open manifest.yaml and find the `selector` field inside the Service spec.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service selector specifies `app: frontend`, but the pod has label `app: backend`. The selector matches zero pods, so the Endpoints list is empty and traffic has nowhere to go.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, under the Service spec, change the selector:

```yaml
spec:
  selector:
    app: backend
    tier: api
```

## Why this matters

Label selectors are how Kubernetes loosely couples Services to Pods — a Service does not reference a pod by name, only by labels. This makes it easy to add or replace pods without touching the Service, but a single typo in a label or selector silently breaks routing.

levels.description: Pod web-server is Running and the Service has endpoints. But connecting to the Service returns 'Connection refused'. Run kubectl get endpoints web-service -n k8smissions to confirm endpoints exist.
levels.hint1:
Endpoints are populated, so the Service found the pod. The problem is somewhere in the port routing. Test the pod directly using port-forward:

  kubectl port-forward pod/web-server 8080:80 -n k8smissions

Then in another terminal: curl http://localhost:8080

Does the pod itself respond?
levels.hint2:
The pod responds when accessed directly. The problem is in how the Service routes traffic to the pod.

Check which port the container is actually listening on:

  kubectl get pod web-server -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'ports'
levels.hint3:
The container declares `containerPort: 80`. Now look at the Service spec:

  kubectl get service web-service -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'ports'

Pay attention to the difference between `port` and `targetPort`.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service has `targetPort: 8080`, but nginx is listening on port 80. When traffic arrives at the Service, it gets forwarded to port 8080 on the pod — where nothing is listening. Connection refused.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, under the Service ports section:

```yaml
ports:
- port: 80
  targetPort: 80
```

## The difference between port and targetPort

- `port` — the port the Service exposes to clients inside the cluster
- `targetPort` — the port on the Pod the traffic is forwarded to (must match containerPort)

levels.description: Pod configmap-pod is stuck in ContainerCreating and never reaches Running. Run kubectl describe pod configmap-pod -n k8smissions to find out why.
levels.hint1:
ContainerCreating that does not progress usually means Kubernetes cannot prepare something the pod needs before starting the container — often a Volume, ConfigMap, or Secret.

Read the Events:

  kubectl describe pod configmap-pod -n k8smissions

Scroll to the bottom and read the Events section.
levels.hint2:
The Events mention a ConfigMap that could not be found. Check if it actually exists:

  kubectl get configmaps -n k8smissions

Is `app-config` in the list?
levels.hint3:
The ConfigMap `app-config` does not exist yet. The pod declares a Volume that references it in spec.volumes.

The solution is to add the missing ConfigMap to manifest.yaml — above the Pod definition, separated by `---`.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod mounts a Volume backed by ConfigMap `app-config`, but this ConfigMap does not exist in the namespace. Kubernetes cannot prepare the Volume, so the container never starts.

## Fix

Add the ConfigMap to manifest.yaml before the Pod:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: k8smissions
data:
  APP_MODE: production
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
# ... rest of pod spec unchanged
```

## Why this matters

Kubernetes prepares all Volumes before starting any container in the pod. If a referenced ConfigMap or Secret is missing, the pod is stuck in ContainerCreating forever — no error in the container logs, because the container never ran.

levels.description: Pod secret-pod shows CreateContainerConfigError and will not start. Run kubectl describe pod secret-pod -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
CreateContainerConfigError means Kubernetes cannot assemble the configuration the container needs before launching it. The error happens before the container runs.

Read the Events:

  kubectl describe pod secret-pod -n k8smissions

What does the Events section say?
levels.hint2:
The Events reference a Secret that could not be found. Check what Secrets exist:

  kubectl get secrets -n k8smissions

Is `db-credentials` in the list?
levels.hint3:
The Secret `db-credentials` does not exist. The pod spec has two env vars that use `secretKeyRef` to pull values from it.

You need to create this Secret and add it to manifest.yaml — above the Pod definition.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod reads `DB_USERNAME` and `DB_PASSWORD` from a Secret named `db-credentials` using `secretKeyRef`. The Secret does not exist, so Kubernetes cannot build the environment for the container.

## Fix

Add the Secret to manifest.yaml before the Pod:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: k8smissions
type: Opaque
stringData:
  username: appuser
  password: mypassword
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
# ... rest of pod spec unchanged
```

## ConfigMap vs Secret

Both inject configuration into pods, but Secret is intended for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys). Kubernetes stores Secret values base64-encoded — this is encoding, not encryption. In production, use a secrets manager like HashiCorp Vault or the External Secrets Operator.

levels.description: Pod app-with-logging has two containers. Run kubectl get pod app-with-logging -n k8smissions and look at the READY column. What does it show?
levels.hint1:
READY shows something like 1/2 — one container is running, the other keeps crashing. Find out which one:

  kubectl describe pod app-with-logging -n k8smissions

In the Containers section, which container has Restart Count > 0?
levels.hint2:
The log-sidecar container is crashing. Read its logs specifically — in a multi-container pod you must use -c to pick the container:

  kubectl logs app-with-logging -n k8smissions -c log-sidecar

What command is the sidecar running and why does it fail?
levels.hint3:
The sidecar runs `tail -f /var/log/nonexistent.log`. The file does not exist in the shared emptyDir volume — `tail` exits with an error immediately.

The fix is in spec.containers[1].command in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The sidecar container runs `tail -f /var/log/nonexistent.log`. The `logs` directory is an emptyDir volume — it starts empty. There is no `nonexistent.log` file, so `tail` exits immediately with an error. The container crashes, Kubernetes restarts it, and the cycle repeats.

## Fix

Change the sidecar command to something that actually writes to the log directory:

```yaml
command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo 'Logging...' >> /var/log/app.log; sleep 5; done"]
```

## Why this matters

In a multi-container pod, all containers must stay running — if one crashes, Kubernetes restarts the entire pod. A sidecar that crashes takes down the healthy main container with it.

levels.description: Pod web-with-init is stuck in Init:0/1 and never reaches Running. Run kubectl get pod web-with-init -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Init:0/1 means the init container has started but not completed yet. The main container will not start until every init container exits successfully.

Read the init container's output:

  kubectl logs web-with-init -n k8smissions -c wait-for-service

What is it waiting for?
levels.hint2:
The init container is running `nslookup backend-service.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local` in a loop. It will only exit when that DNS name resolves.

Check if that Service exists:

  kubectl get services -n k8smissions

Is `backend-service` there?
levels.hint3:
The init container is waiting for `backend-service` — which does not exist. The init container itself is not the problem. The dependency it is waiting for is the problem.

Rather than removing the init container, look at what you need to add to manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The init container waits until `backend-service.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local` resolves in DNS. That Service does not exist, so the init container loops forever and the main container never starts.

## Fix

Add the missing Service to manifest.yaml before the Pod:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: backend-service
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  selector:
    app: backend
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
# ... rest of pod spec unchanged
```

## Why this matters

Using an init container to wait for a dependency is a legitimate Kubernetes pattern — it enforces startup ordering. The mistake here was not the pattern but the missing dependency it was checking for.

levels.description: Pod oom-pod keeps restarting. Run kubectl get pod oom-pod -n k8smissions and note the STATUS and RESTARTS values.
levels.hint1:
Multiple restarts with no obvious crash message usually means the container was killed by the OS, not by the application itself. Get the details of the last termination:

  kubectl describe pod oom-pod -n k8smissions

Look at the 'Last State' section under the container.
levels.hint2:
Last State shows Reason: OOMKilled and Exit Code: 137. The Linux kernel killed the container because it exceeded its memory limit.

Now check what the memory limit is:

  kubectl get pod oom-pod -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A8 'resources'
levels.hint3:
The memory limit is 64Mi. The Python program allocates 128 arrays of 1MB each — that is about 128MB. The container hits the limit and gets killed.

Find spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`limits.memory: 64Mi` — the container is allowed to use at most 64 megabytes. The Python program allocates ~128MB, exceeds the limit, and the Linux kernel sends SIGKILL (exit code 137, also shown as OOMKilled).

## Fix

Increase the memory limit in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: 256Mi
  requests:
    memory: 64Mi
```

## Why this matters

OOMKilled is one of the most common production issues in Kubernetes. Set limits high enough to survive peak usage. Use `kubectl top pod` to observe actual memory consumption before deciding on the right limit.

levels.description: Pod database-app starts and exits repeatedly. The logs are the only clue. Run kubectl logs database-app -n k8smissions and read the full output.
levels.hint1:
When an application crashes at startup without an obvious Kubernetes error, the answer is almost always in the application logs. Read them:

  kubectl logs database-app -n k8smissions

If the container already restarted:

  kubectl logs database-app -n k8smissions --previous

Read the PostgreSQL output carefully.
levels.hint2:
PostgreSQL is printing an error about a missing required setting and refusing to start. Check what environment variables are currently set in the pod:

  kubectl get pod database-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A10 'env'

Is there an `env` section at all?
levels.hint3:
There is no `env` section. PostgreSQL requires at least one specific environment variable to be set before it will start. The log message names it directly.

Add the missing variable to spec.containers[0] in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The official PostgreSQL image requires `POSTGRES_PASSWORD` to be set before it starts. This is a deliberate safety check — PostgreSQL refuses to run with an empty superuser password. Since no env vars are defined, it exits on every start.

## Fix

Add the required environment variable in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
env:
- name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
  value: "mysecretpassword"
```

## Why this matters

This level teaches the most important debugging habit: read the application logs first. The log said exactly what was missing. Skipping the logs and guessing the problem wastes time — the application already told you the answer.

levels.description: Pod selector-pod has been Pending since it was created. No node was assigned. Run kubectl describe pod selector-pod -n k8smissions and read the Events.
levels.hint1:
The scheduler explains why it could not place the pod. Look at Events:

  kubectl describe pod selector-pod -n k8smissions

Find the line that says 'node(s) didn't match' or similar. What constraint could not be satisfied?
levels.hint2:
The scheduler says no node matches the nodeSelector. Check what labels your nodes actually have:

  kubectl get nodes --show-labels

Look through the labels carefully. Is `mission=moon-base` present on any node?
levels.hint3:
No node has the label `mission=moon-base`. The nodeSelector in the pod spec requires this label to exist on a node before the pod can be scheduled.

Look at spec.nodeSelector in manifest.yaml. You need to change it to a label that actually exists on a node.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`nodeSelector: {mission: moon-base}` — no node in the cluster has this label. The scheduler checked every node and found no match, so the pod stays Pending indefinitely.

## Fix

Change the nodeSelector to a label that exists on your nodes:

```yaml
nodeSelector:
  kubernetes.io/os: linux
```

This label is automatically applied to every Linux node by Kubernetes itself.

## Why this matters

nodeSelector is used to restrict a pod to specific nodes — for example nodes with GPUs, high memory, or in a specific availability zone. If the label does not exist, the pod will never run. Always verify labels with `kubectl get nodes --show-labels` before adding a nodeSelector.

levels.description: DaemonSet log-collector should run one pod on every node, but its pods are stuck in Pending. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -o wide to see which nodes they were assigned to.
levels.hint1:
DaemonSet pods are not being scheduled. When a pod cannot be scheduled, it is almost always because of node constraints. Check what taints the nodes have:

  kubectl describe node | grep -A3 'Taints'

What taint is present? What does it mean for scheduling?
levels.hint2:
The control-plane node has a taint `node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule`. This taint repels any pod that does not explicitly tolerate it.

Check whether the DaemonSet spec has any tolerations:

  kubectl get daemonset log-collector -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A8 'tolerations'
levels.hint3:
The DaemonSet has no tolerations. It needs to tolerate the control-plane taint to be scheduled on that node.

The `tolerations` field goes under spec.template.spec in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The control-plane node carries the taint `node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule`. This tells the scheduler: 'do not place pods here unless they explicitly tolerate this taint'. The DaemonSet has no tolerations, so its pod is rejected from the only available node.

## Fix

Add tolerations under spec.template.spec in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
tolerations:
- key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
  operator: Exists
  effect: NoSchedule
- key: node-role.kubernetes.io/master
  operator: Exists
  effect: NoSchedule
```

## Why this matters

DaemonSets are typically used for cluster-wide components like log collectors, monitoring agents, and network plugins. These must run on every node — including control-plane nodes. Without the right tolerations, they silently miss one or more nodes.

levels.description: Job db-init has reached BackoffLimitExceeded. Run kubectl get jobs -n k8smissions to see its current state.
levels.hint1:
BackoffLimitExceeded means the Job tried to complete and failed every time, hitting the maximum number of retries. Find the pods the Job created:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l job-name=db-init

What is the status of those pods?
levels.hint2:
The pods are in Error state. Read the logs from one of them:

  kubectl logs -n k8smissions -l job-name=db-init

What command ran? What exit code did it produce and why?
levels.hint3:
The command is `/scripts/init-db.sh`. This script does not exist inside the busybox:1.36 image — it exits with code 127 (command not found).

Look at spec.template.spec.containers[0].command in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Job runs `/scripts/init-db.sh` inside a busybox container. The script does not exist in the image. The container exits with code 127 immediately. Kubernetes retries up to `backoffLimit: 2` times, then marks the Job as Failed.

## Fix

Replace the command with one that succeeds:

```yaml
command:
- /bin/sh
- -c
- echo 'DB initialised'; exit 0
```

## Why this matters

A Kubernetes Job considers any exit code other than 0 a failure and retries the pod. Only exit code 0 marks the Job as Succeeded. This is by design — Jobs are built for tasks that must complete successfully exactly once.

levels.description: CronJob report-generator runs on a schedule of '* * * * *' (every minute) but has never created a single Job. Run kubectl get cronjobs -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
In the output of get cronjobs, look at the SUSPEND and LAST SCHEDULE columns. Then get more detail:

  kubectl describe cronjob report-generator -n k8smissions

Pay attention to the Suspend field and whether any Jobs have been created.
levels.hint2:
No Jobs have been created at all. Confirm this:

  kubectl get jobs -n k8smissions

Is there anything there? What does the Suspend field tell you about why the CronJob is not running?
levels.hint3: The CronJob is in a suspended state. Look at `spec.suspend` in manifest.yaml. This single field controls whether the CronJob is allowed to fire.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`spec.suspend: true` puts the CronJob into a paused state. Kubernetes will not create any new Jobs from it until suspend is set to false.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
suspend: false
```

## Why this matters

`suspend` exists so you can temporarily stop a CronJob without deleting it — useful during maintenance windows or while debugging a failing schedule. If you inherit a CronJob that is never running, checking `suspend` is the first thing to do.

levels.description: Pod noisy-pod keeps getting OOMKilled, but there is no memory limit defined in its spec. Run kubectl get pod noisy-pod -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
A pod with no explicit memory limit should not be OOMKilled unless something else is injecting a limit. Check if the namespace has a LimitRange:

  kubectl get limitrange -n k8smissions

Does anything show up?
levels.hint2:
A LimitRange exists. Get its details:

  kubectl describe limitrange -n k8smissions

What is the default memory limit it injects into containers? How much memory does the program actually use?
levels.hint3:
The LimitRange sets a default memory limit of 32Mi. The program allocates 64MB. Because there is no explicit limit in the pod spec, the LimitRange default is applied and the container gets OOMKilled.

To override the default, add an explicit `resources` section to the pod in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The namespace has a LimitRange that automatically injects `limits.memory: 32Mi` into any container that does not specify its own. The Python program allocates `bytearray(64 * 1024 * 1024)` — 64MB — which exceeds the injected 32Mi limit. OOMKilled.

## Fix

Add explicit resource limits to the pod in manifest.yaml to override the LimitRange default:

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    memory: 64Mi
  limits:
    memory: 128Mi
```

## Why this matters

LimitRange is a namespace-level admission policy set by cluster administrators. It silently applies to every pod that does not specify its own limits. When a pod is OOMKilled but has no visible limit in its spec, always check `kubectl describe limitrange -n <namespace>` first.

levels.description: ConfigMap stuck-config is in Terminating state and has been for several minutes. Run kubectl get configmap stuck-config -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
A resource stuck in Terminating means `kubectl delete` was called but Kubernetes cannot finish deleting it. Something is blocking removal. Get the details:

  kubectl describe configmap stuck-config -n k8smissions

Look at the Finalizers field.
levels.hint2:
There is a finalizer: `custom.operator.io/cleanup`. A finalizer tells Kubernetes: 'before you delete this object, wait for an external process to signal that cleanup is done'.

Check if that process exists anywhere in the cluster:

  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces | grep operator

Is there anything running that belongs to `custom.operator.io`?
levels.hint3:
The operator that should clear this finalizer does not exist in the cluster. Kubernetes will wait forever.

This level cannot be solved by editing manifest.yaml and applying it — the object is already being deleted. You need a different kubectl command to remove the finalizer directly.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The finalizer `custom.operator.io/cleanup` is registered on the ConfigMap. Kubernetes holds off on deleting the object until some operator removes the finalizer — signalling that cleanup is done. That operator does not exist, so the ConfigMap is stuck in Terminating forever.

## Fix

Remove the finalizer directly using kubectl patch:

```bash
kubectl patch configmap stuck-config -n k8smissions \
  --type=json \
  -p='[{"op": "remove", "path": "/metadata/finalizers"}]'
```

Once the finalizer list is empty, Kubernetes immediately completes the deletion.

## Why this matters

Finalizers are a legitimate Kubernetes mechanism — for example, a storage driver uses them to ensure a PersistentVolume is released before the claim is deleted. When the responsible controller crashes or is removed while finalizers remain on objects, those objects get stuck. Manually patching out the finalizer is the standard recovery procedure.
